output.final_deliverable:
Missing evidence: the merged results contain only search result listings (titles/URLs/snippets) and no visited-page extracts. The mandate requires visiting sources and using only evidence from those visits, with inline URL citations. With the current merged results alone, I can’t provide a compliant, evidence-grounded synthesis across the 5 domains.

What I need to do next to complete the deliverable (per the benchmark rule):
- Quantum computing: visit (at least) a NIST overview PDF on qubit modalities and fundamentals, plus a peer‑reviewed NISQ/capabilities paper (e.g., Nature Communications).
- Cryptography/PQC: visit NIST CSRC pages for PQC FIPS approvals and the PQC standardization page (and/or NIST IR transition guidance).
- Climate/infrastructure: visit Uptime Institute’s extreme-weather/data-center resilience analysis plus an energy/water/climate risk report/article with concrete metrics.
- Economics: visit BCG’s long-term forecast and a McKinsey Quantum Technology Monitor page for quantified projections.
- Ethics: visit a peer‑reviewed/open-access ethics/governance paper (e.g., PMC) plus a legal/policy framework piece (e.g., Yale JOLT).

Candidate authoritative sources already identified in the merged search results (to be visited):
1) Quantum computing principles/capabilities
- NIST PDF (qubit types: trapped ions, spins, superconducting circuits): https://tf.nist.gov/general/pdf/3119.pdf
- Nature Communications on defining/characterizing NISQ complexity/capabilities: https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-023-41217-6

2) Cryptography/PQC
- NIST CSRC PQC FIPS approved announcement: https://csrc.nist.gov/news/2024/postquantum-cryptography-fips-approved
- NIST PQC standardization project page (FIPS 203–205 publication date; Falcon upcoming): https://csrc.nist.gov/projects/post-quantum-cryptography/post-quantum-cryptography-standardization
- NIST news release on finalized standards: https://www.nist.gov/news-events/news/2024/08/nist-releases-first-3-finalized-post-quantum-encryption-standards

3) Climate change impacts on computing infrastructure
- Uptime Institute on extreme weather affecting data centers: https://journal.uptimeinstitute.com/extreme-weather-affects-nearly-half-of-data-centers/
- S&P Global Sustainable1 on water stress and cooling energy shares (references IEA Energy and AI report): https://www.spglobal.com/sustainable1/en/insights/special-editorial/beneath-the-surface-water-stress-in-data-centers

4) Economic implications
- BCG forecast ($450B–$850B economic value by 2040; provider market size): https://www.bcg.com/publications/2024/long-term-forecast-for-quantum-computing-still-looks-bright
- McKinsey “The rise of quantum computing” (market to $100B in a decade—needs verification on-page): https://www.mckinsey.com/featured-insights/the-rise-of-quantum-computing

5) Ethical considerations
- Peer-reviewed open-access ethics/governance continuity framing: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12518464/
- Yale JOLT legal-ethical framework (inequality/monopolization/“quantum divide”): https://yjolt.org/blog/establishing-legal-ethical-framework-quantum-technology

If you want, I can proceed to visit these pages (and any alternates you prefer) and then produce the comprehensive, cross-domain synthesis with at least two authoritative, visited sources per domain and inline citations to the visited URLs.
output.action_summary: The merged results only include search listings; no pages were visited/extracted. Because the instructions require using only evidence from visits with URL citations, the requested 5-domain synthesis cannot be completed yet. I listed the best candidate authoritative sources (already found) that must be visited next to generate a compliant, cited analysis.

61423780-4862-4ff7-8cb9-1a81b6da3c52.title:
Research and synthesize information across 5 domains: (1) Quantum computing principles and current capabilities, (2) Cryptography and encryption methods, (3) Climate change impact on computing infrastructure, (4) Economic implications of quantum computing adoption, and (5) Ethical considerations of quantum supremacy. Then create a comprehensive analysis explaining how these domains interconnect, what conflicts or synergies exist, and what the future landscape might look like. Each domain must have at least 2 authoritative sources. The synthesis must demonstrate deep understanding of cross-domain relationships.

Benchmark rule: when external evidence is needed, run search then visit the best URLs. Visit pages to extract concrete details and cite visited URLs in the final answer.

details.mandate:
Research and synthesize information across 5 domains: (1) Quantum computing principles and current capabilities, (2) Cryptography and encryption methods, (3) Climate change impact on computing infrastructure, (4) Economic implications of quantum computing adoption, and (5) Ethical considerations of quantum supremacy. Then create a comprehensive analysis explaining how these domains interconnect, what conflicts or synergies exist, and what the future landscape might look like. merged_results.title: Gather authoritative sources on quantum computing principles + current capabilities (hardware modalities, error correction, current milestones, performance limits)

result.query: authoritative overview quantum computing principles current capabilities superconducting trapped ion error correction NISQ review site:ibm.com OR site:nist.gov OR site:nature.com OR site:science.org
result.intent: Find 2+ authoritative sources explaining quantum computing principles (qubits, superposition, entanglement), hardware approaches, and current real-world capabilities/limitations; identify pages worth visiting for concrete details and citations.

results.title: The complexity of NISQ | Nature Communications
results.description: Our current understanding of the computational abilities of near-intermediate scale quantum (NISQ) computing devices is limited, in part due to the absence of a precise definition for this regime. Here, the authors formally define the NISQ realm and provide rigorous evidence that its capabilities are situated between the complexity classes BPP and BQP.

results.title: Hardware-efficient quantum error correction via concatenated bosonic qubits | Nature
results.description: Recently, QEC experiments have been performed in various hardware platforms, including superconducting quantum circuits25,26,27,28, trapped ions29 and neutral atoms30. Some of these experiments are approaching26, or have surpassed28, the threshold at which scaling of the error-correcting code size leads to exponential improvements in the logical qubit error rate.

results.title: Harnessing quantum: progress towards real world applications of quantum technologies
results.description: Here, the authors conclude that analogue quantum simulators are robust to errors and can provide superpolynomial to exponential quantum advantage when used to compute relevant many-body observables.

results.title: Received 16 September 2020; revised 20 October 2020; accepted 22 October 2020.
results.description: tions of qubits: trapped atomic ions, spins in semiconductors, and superconducting circuits. Below, we brieﬂy explain the · fundamentals and properties of these different types of qubits, all of which can have ω01 in the microwave region of the ... VOLUME 1, NO. 1, JANUARY 2021 · FIGURE 3. Relevant ranges of the electromagnetic spectrum for quantum computing.

results.title: Materials challenges for trapped-ion quantum computers | Nature Reviews Materials
results.description: Trapped-ion quantum information processors store information in atomic ions maintained in position in free space by electric fields. Quantum logic is enacted through manipulation of the ions’ internal and shared motional quantum states using ...

results.title: Dissipative quantum error correction and application to quantum sensing with trapped ions | Nature Communications
results.description: Quantum error correction plays ... sequences. Here, the authors use trapped ions to implement a scheme in which always-on coupling to an engineered environment protects the qubit against errors....

results.title: Extending the computational reach of a superconducting qutrit processor | npj Quantum Information
results.description: In the noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) era1, the ability to effectively couple many quantum two-level systems (qubits) together has led to experimental demonstrations of certain tasks that challenge the limits of current classical capabilities2,3,4,5. The computational power of these near-term devices can potentially be further boosted by leveraging the innate multi-level structure to encode quantum information in the larger and more connected Hilbert space of d-level systems (qudits)6,7,8,9,10,11. Coherent control of higher-level quantum systems has been demonstrated in superconducting circuits12,13,14,15,16,17,18, trapped ions19,20, and in photonic circuits21,22.

results.title: Single-Electron Spin Qubits in Silicon for Quantum Computing | Intelligent Computing
results.description: Now, we are in the noisy intermediate scale quantum (NISQ) era [6]. Superconducting and photonics platforms have showcased quantum advantages [7–12], and superconducting qubits are already on the verge of a break-even point for fault-tolerant logic qubits [13–15]. Several applications, including topological braiding operations [16], solving computationally complex problems in the analog quantum simulation approach [17], and time crystals [18], have already been demonstrated. Recently, trapped-ion and neutral-atom systems have pushed quantum gate fidelities and qubit numbers to higher levels [19,20]. These advancements have bolstered confidence in realizing a universal quantum computer, a previously formidable goal that now appears reachable.

merged_results.title: Gather authoritative sources on cryptography/encryption and quantum threat + post-quantum cryptography (PQC) standards and timelines

result.query: NIST post-quantum cryptography standardization FIPS 203 204 205 quantum threat Shor algorithm RSA ECC authoritative guidance
result.intent: Find 2+ authoritative sources on modern public-key cryptography, quantum attacks (Shor/Grover), and PQC standardization (NIST/FIPS) with concrete migration guidance and dates to cite.

results.title: NIST Releases First 3 Finalized Post-Quantum Encryption Standards | NIST
results.description: Federal Information Processing Standard (FIPS) 203, intended as the primary standard for general encryption. Among its advantages are comparatively small encryption keys that two parties can exchange easily, as well as its speed of operation.

results.title: A Complete Guide to Post-Quantum Cryptography Standards - Palo Alto Networks
results.description: Key standards include FIPS 203–205, SP 800‑208, SP 800‑227, ISO/IEC 23837-1, ETSI TS 103 744, and RFC 9794. PQC readiness refers to an organization’s preparedness to adopt post-quantum cryptography.

results.title: Diving Into NIST’s New Post-Quantum Standards
results.description: It efficiently solves the mathematical problems underlying the most common forms of asymmetric cryptography, RSA and ECC, rendering them breakable by a sufficiently powerful quantum computer.

results.title: Post-Quantum Cryptography FIPS Approved - NIST CSRC
results.description: FIPS 203 specifies a cryptographic scheme called the Module-Lattice-Based Key-Encapsulation Mechanism Standard, which is derived from the CRYSTALS-KYBER submission. A key encapsulation mechanism (KEM) is a particular type of key establishment scheme that can be used to establish a shared secret ...

results.title: PQC Standardization Process - Post-Quantum Cryptography | CSRC | CSRC
results.description: Short URL: https://csrc.nist.gov/pqc-standardization FIPS 203, FIPS 204, and FIPS 205, which specify algorithms derived from CRYSTALS-Dilithium, CRYSTALS-KYBER, and SPHINCS+, were published August 13, 2024. FALCON was also selected and will be published in FIPS 206 (in development).

results.title: NIST FIPS 203, 204, 205 Finalized | PQC Algorithms | CSA
results.description: Summary: This standard describes a set of algorithms known as module-lattice-key-encapsulation mechanism (ML-KEM). These algorithms should be used in conjunction with symmetric-key cryptographic algorithms for quantum-safe data encryption. They work by leveraging the strength and computational ...

results.title: NIST Internal Report NIST IR 8547 ipd Transition to Post-Quantum
results.description: cryptography (ECC) based key establishment schemes in SP 800-56A [SP80056A]. These · algorithms are vulnerable to Shor’s Algorithm on a cryptographically relevant quantum ... FIPS 204 and 205 each specify quantum-resistant digital signature schemes.

results.title: Post-Quantum Cryptography - NIST CSRC
results.description: NIST continues to evaluate the security and performance of innovative algorithms in order to identify additional candidates for standardization. In addition to the three algorithms specified in the initial FIPS standards, the Falcon digital signature algorithm and HQC key encapsulation mechanism were selected for ongoing standardization; that process is underway.

merged_results.title: Gather authoritative sources on climate change impacts to computing infrastructure (data centers, energy/water use, heat, flooding, resilience)

result.query: climate change impacts data centers computing infrastructure heat water risk flooding resilience authoritative report IEA Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory Uptime Institute
result.intent: Find 2+ authoritative sources quantifying climate risks and adaptation needs for computing infrastructure (data centers, grids, cooling, water), with concrete metrics and recommendations.

results.title: Extreme weather affects nearly half of data centers - Uptime Institute Blog
results.description: Any investments in resiliency need, of course, to be based on sound risk analysis. Uptime Institute strongly recommends that operators conduct regular reviews of climate change-related risks to their data centers.

results.title: Climate threats to data centers set to surge - report - DCD
results.description: Climate risks, which are considered at a very high level of concern by the report, include riverine flooding, which risks severe damage to electronics, walls, roofs, and power systems from water ingress, and coastal inundation, which risks buildings being flooded, leading to water damage to cabling.

results.title: Data Centers Are Facing a Climate Crisis | WIRED
results.description: Forty-five percent of US data centers have experienced an extreme weather event that threatened their ability to operate, according to a survey by the Uptime Institute, a digital services standards agency.

results.title: Desert storm: Can data centres slake their insatiable thirst for water? | Reuters
results.description: The average mid-size data centre uses about 1.4 million litres of water a day for cooling servers, according to Verisk Maplecroft, and these requirements will increase as the climate gets hotter.

results.title: Beneath the surface: Water stress in data centers | S&P Global
results.description: Most existing DCs, which mainly serve cloud computing and storage, use water-cooled chillers (worst WUE shown in chart 5). Evaporative cooling is more energy efficient than air cooling, but it requires large amounts of water and is the main source of water consumption for existing DCs. Despite the rapid build-out of new DCs, such older facilities form the backbone of the industry. DC scale influences water consumption. According to the IEA 2025 Energy and AI Report, the share of cooling system energy usage as a share of total energy consumption ranges from approximately 7% for efficient hyperscale DCs to over 30% for less efficient enterprise DCs.

results.title: Data centers, backbone of the digital economy, face water scarcity and climate risk
results.description: Data centers have become integral to a global economy that's powered by digital information. However, many of the facilities depend on water to keep from overheating. That is further straining water resources in places like California, where Lake Oroville is almost dry due to severe drought that's being fueled by climate change.

results.title: 2025 Global Data Centre Physical Climate Risk and Adaptation Report July 2025
results.description: as average MVAR. The green column shows how this risk is reduced when data centre infrastructure is ... Lower resilience vs. ... Lower resilience vs. ... Coastal Inundation. ... Water Flooding the driving hazard.

results.title: The data center boom threatens climate goals. How to fight back | Trellis
results.description: Ohio, for example, has become a hotbed for expansion — AWS alone is spending $10 billion in the state — largely because American Electric Power has made substantial investments in transmission infrastructure, which has been a sticking point for other regions, Lash said. Geography also matters from a resilience perspective. As the climate warms, that’s a consideration for cooling systems — which can demand a lot of both power and water. Picking a location that’s at lower risk of flooding and natural disasters is also essential.

merged_results.title: Gather authoritative sources on economic implications of quantum computing adoption (industry value, investment, cost curves, deployment models)

result.query: economic impact of quantum computing adoption report McKinsey BCG OECD World Economic Forum quantum computing economics investment forecast
result.intent: Find 2+ authoritative sources on projected economic value, adoption pathways, costs/risks, sector impacts; identify data points to ground synthesis.

results.title: Quantum Computing On Track to Create Up to $850 Billion of Economic Value By 2040
results.description: In its updated analysis titled, The Long-Term Forecast for Quantum Computing Still Looks Bright , BCG reaffirms its projection that quantum computing will create $450 billion to $850 billion of economic value globally, sustaining a $90 billion ...

results.title: The Long-Term Forecast for Quantum Computing Still Looks Bright
results.description: We continue to have confidence in our projection that quantum computing will create $450 billion to $850 billion of economic value—sustaining a $90 billion to $170 billion market for hardware and software providers—by 2040.

results.title: Quantum computing to create over $450 billion of economic value by 2040
results.description: An analysis from competitor McKinsey in their 2023 Quantum Technology Monitor estimates that the industries likely to benefit most from quantum computing stand to gain up to $2 trillion in value by 2035. In terms of progress of quantum technology since 2021, many milestones have been hit in ...

results.title: Quantum Computing Valuation: Navigating the Hype and the Future | SpinQ
results.description: The most optimistic scenario comes from McKinsey & Company, which forecasts that quantum technologies could generate up to $97 billion in revenue worldwide by 2035, with quantum computing capturing the bulk of that revenue at approximately $72 billion.

results.title: The Rise of Quantum Computing | McKinsey & Company
results.description: June 23, 2025 – Our fourth annual Quantum Technology Monitor report shows that surging investment and faster-than-expected innovation could propel the quantum market to $100 billion in a decade.

results.title: Quantum Computing On Track to Create Up to $850 Billion of Economic Value By 2040
results.description: In its updated analysis titled, ... that quantum computing will create $450 billion to $850 billion of economic value globally, sustaining a $90 billion to $170 billion market for hardware and software providers by 2040...

results.title: McKinsey & Co. New Report on Quantum Computing – Becoming a Reality! | Tony's Thoughts
results.description: McKinsey & Co. has just published a new report on advances in quantum computing that shows investment could propel the quantum market to $100 billion in a decade. Just as AI is changing the software aspect of digital technology, quantum computing has the potential to do the same for the hardware ...

results.description: Public funding hit $10 billion by April, spiked by investment annoucements of $7.4 billion from Japan and $900 million from Spain (Exhibit 2). Other national announcements include Australia’s $620 million commitment to creating the world’s ...

merged_results.title: Gather authoritative sources on ethical considerations of quantum supremacy/advantage (security, inequality, governance, dual-use, responsible innovation)

result.query: ethical considerations quantum supremacy governance dual use inequality responsible innovation report UNESCO OECD quantum technology ethics
result.intent: Find 2+ authoritative sources on ethical, societal, and governance issues around quantum advantage/supremacy and its externalities; identify concrete principles/policy proposals to cite.

results.title: From Nano to Quantum: Ethics Through a Lens of Continuity - PMC
results.description: As with nanotechnology, the prevailing attitude towards quantum technology is to “get it right from the outset.” This is reflected in efforts toward anticipatory governance (OECD, 2025) and, specifically, ‘upstream ethics’ (de Jong, 2025). However, engaging in ethics upstream is inherently ...

results.title: Establishing a Legal-Ethical Framework for Quantum Technology | Yale Journal of Law & Technology
results.description: These horizontal rules should be ... societal risks identified to date are: [51] Risks of increased inequality, monopolization through IP, winner-takes-all effects, and a “quantum divide” during the introductory phase....

results.title: The Risk of Ethicalisation in Ethical Engagement with Quantum Technologies: Some Brief Considerations | Ethics and Society | Springer Nature Link
results.description: For instance, the World Economic Forum highlights the importance of orienting the governance of quantum computing towards the ‘benefit of humanity’ [6]. At national level, the Dutch quantum innovation system emphasises ‘quantum for good’ as a key driver [7]. At a conceptual level, ‘quantum for good’ can be viewed as a signifier (or container) for a certain direction of technology development.

results.title: The Ethical Dilemmas Of The Quantum Age
results.description: Still, initiatives such as the ... continue to evolve, we must prioritize responsible innovation and ensure that their development and use are aligned with human values and principles....

results.title: Governments Face Key Challenges in Quantum Technology Policy, OECD Report Finds
results.description: The report, A Quantum Technologies Policy Primer, outlines the transformative potential of quantum computing, sensing, and communication, but warns that inadequate governance could slow progress and deepen global inequalities.

results.title: Ethical Implications of Quantum Computing in the Modern ...
results.description: technological inequality, and job displacement. It is essential for researchers, policymakers, and industry leaders to · collaborate and establish clear ethical frameworks to guide the development and deployment of quantum technologies. Ensuring that quantum computing benefits society as a whole will require proactive governance, international

results.title: Quantum for Whom? Situating Ethical Risks in Quantum Technologies’ Innovation Context | Ethics and Society | Springer Nature Link
results.description: Relatedly, recent work highlights how framing ethical risk primarily in terms of malicious misuse amplifies hype and fearmongering, thereby inflating threat perceptions while diverting attention from the responsibilities embedded in design, governance, and innovation practices [53]. Quantum’s strategic positioning makes it prone to such ambiguous claims, with scientific and technological milestones often serving as measures of competition between institutions, firms, and states [9]. A prominent example is the discourse surrounding “quantum supremacy,” following Google’s 2019 announcement [54], which, despite its experimental specificity, was widely interpreted as signalling imminent, general-purpose quantum advantage.

results.title: Quantum Ethics: Why We Must Plan for a Responsible Quantum Future
results.description: This growing body of research will inform and refine the practical guidelines adopted by industry and government. Crucially, it brings together experts across disciplines – quantum physics, computer science, ethics, law, social science – reflecting the understanding that quantum ethics requires a holistic, multidisciplinary approach. Overall, these efforts show a consensus that “with quantum power comes quantum responsibility.” Organizations are starting to articulate what responsible innovation should look like in this domain.

action_result.intent: Find 2+ authoritative sources quantifying climate risks and adaptation needs for computing infrastructure (data centers, grids, cooling, water), with concrete metrics and recommendations.

results.title: Extreme weather affects nearly half of data centers - Uptime Institute Blog
results.description: Any investments in resiliency need, of course, to be based on sound risk analysis. Uptime Institute strongly recommends that operators conduct regular reviews of climate change-related risks to their data centers.

results.title: Climate threats to data centers set to surge - report - DCD
results.description: Climate risks, which are considered at a very high level of concern by the report, include riverine flooding, which risks severe damage to electronics, walls, roofs, and power systems from water ingress, and coastal inundation, which risks buildings being flooded, leading to water damage to cabling.

results.title: Data Centers Are Facing a Climate Crisis | WIRED
results.description: Forty-five percent of US data centers have experienced an extreme weather event that threatened their ability to operate, according to a survey by the Uptime Institute, a digital services standards agency.

results.title: Desert storm: Can data centres slake their insatiable thirst for water? | Reuters
results.description: The average mid-size data centre uses about 1.4 million litres of water a day for cooling servers, according to Verisk Maplecroft, and these requirements will increase as the climate gets hotter.

results.title: Beneath the surface: Water stress in data centers | S&P Global
results.description: Most existing DCs, which mainly serve cloud computing and storage, use water-cooled chillers (worst WUE shown in chart 5). Evaporative cooling is more energy efficient than air cooling, but it requires large amounts of water and is the main source of water consumption for existing DCs. Despite the rapid build-out of new DCs, such older facilities form the backbone of the industry. DC scale influences water consumption. According to the IEA 2025 Energy and AI Report, the share of cooling system energy usage as a share of total energy consumption ranges from approximately 7% for efficient hyperscale DCs to over 30% for less efficient enterprise DCs.

results.title: Data centers, backbone of the digital economy, face water scarcity and climate risk
results.description: Data centers have become integral to a global economy that's powered by digital information. However, many of the facilities depend on water to keep from overheating. That is further straining water resources in places like California, where Lake Oroville is almost dry due to severe drought that's being fueled by climate change.

results.title: 2025 Global Data Centre Physical Climate Risk and Adaptation Report July 2025
results.description: as average MVAR. The green column shows how this risk is reduced when data centre infrastructure is ... Lower resilience vs. ... Lower resilience vs. ... Coastal Inundation. ... Water Flooding the driving hazard.

results.title: The data center boom threatens climate goals. How to fight back | Trellis
results.description: Ohio, for example, has become a hotbed for expansion — AWS alone is spending $10 billion in the state — largely because American Electric Power has made substantial investments in transmission infrastructure, which has been a sticking point for other regions, Lash said. Geography also matters from a resilience perspective. As the climate warms, that’s a consideration for cooling systems — which can demand a lot of both power and water. Picking a location that’s at lower risk of flooding and natural disasters is also essential.

results.title: From Nano to Quantum: Ethics Through a Lens of Continuity - PMC
results.description: As with nanotechnology, the prevailing attitude towards quantum technology is to “get it right from the outset.” This is reflected in efforts toward anticipatory governance (OECD, 2025) and, specifically, ‘upstream ethics’ (de Jong, 2025). However, engaging in ethics upstream is inherently ...

results.title: Establishing a Legal-Ethical Framework for Quantum Technology | Yale Journal of Law & Technology
results.description: These horizontal rules should be ... societal risks identified to date are: [51] Risks of increased inequality, monopolization through IP, winner-takes-all effects, and a “quantum divide” during the introductory phase....

results.title: The Risk of Ethicalisation in Ethical Engagement with Quantum Technologies: Some Brief Considerations | Ethics and Society | Springer Nature Link
results.description: For instance, the World Economic Forum highlights the importance of orienting the governance of quantum computing towards the ‘benefit of humanity’ [6]. At national level, the Dutch quantum innovation system emphasises ‘quantum for good’ as a key driver [7]. At a conceptual level, ‘quantum for good’ can be viewed as a signifier (or container) for a certain direction of technology development.

results.title: The Ethical Dilemmas Of The Quantum Age
results.description: Still, initiatives such as the ... continue to evolve, we must prioritize responsible innovation and ensure that their development and use are aligned with human values and principles....

results.title: Governments Face Key Challenges in Quantum Technology Policy, OECD Report Finds
results.description: The report, A Quantum Technologies Policy Primer, outlines the transformative potential of quantum computing, sensing, and communication, but warns that inadequate governance could slow progress and deepen global inequalities.

results.title: Ethical Implications of Quantum Computing in the Modern ...
results.description: technological inequality, and job displacement. It is essential for researchers, policymakers, and industry leaders to · collaborate and establish clear ethical frameworks to guide the development and deployment of quantum technologies. Ensuring that quantum computing benefits society as a whole will require proactive governance, international

results.title: Quantum for Whom? Situating Ethical Risks in Quantum Technologies’ Innovation Context | Ethics and Society | Springer Nature Link
results.description: Relatedly, recent work highlights how framing ethical risk primarily in terms of malicious misuse amplifies hype and fearmongering, thereby inflating threat perceptions while diverting attention from the responsibilities embedded in design, governance, and innovation practices [53]. Quantum’s strategic positioning makes it prone to such ambiguous claims, with scientific and technological milestones often serving as measures of competition between institutions, firms, and states [9]. A prominent example is the discourse surrounding “quantum supremacy,” following Google’s 2019 announcement [54], which, despite its experimental specificity, was widely interpreted as signalling imminent, general-purpose quantum advantage.

results.title: Quantum Ethics: Why We Must Plan for a Responsible Quantum Future
results.description: This growing body of research will inform and refine the practical guidelines adopted by industry and government. Crucially, it brings together experts across disciplines – quantum physics, computer science, ethics, law, social science – reflecting the understanding that quantum ethics requires a holistic, multidisciplinary approach. Overall, these efforts show a consensus that “with quantum power comes quantum responsibility.” Organizations are starting to articulate what responsible innovation should look like in this domain.
